questions.text: What gives lobster blood its bluish color when oxygenated?
questions.explanation: Lobsters and other crustaceans use copper-based hemocyanin to transport oxygen.

questions.text: In which decade was the modern wristwatch first widely adopted by men?
questions.explanation: Soldiers in the trenches needed hands-free timekeeping — pocket watches were impractical.

questions.text: A 'jiffy' is an actual unit of time used in physics.
questions.explanation: In physics, a jiffy can mean the time light takes to travel one centimeter (~33 picoseconds).

questions.text: What is the only letter that doesn't appear in any U.S. state name?
questions.explanation: Every other letter shows up somewhere — but no state name contains a Q.

questions.text: What does the 'M' stand for in M&M's?
questions.explanation: Forrest Mars Sr. and Bruce Murrie struck a partnership in 1941.

questions.text: How many ridges are on the edge of a U.S. quarter?
questions.explanation: Exactly 119, originally to prevent shaving precious metal off coins.

questions.text: The dot above a lowercase 'i' or 'j' has a name.
questions.explanation: It's called a 'tittle'.

questions.text: What is the loneliest whale, found because of its unusually high call frequency?
questions.explanation: It vocalizes at 52 Hz, well above the typical 10–40 Hz of other baleen whales.

questions.text: Which is the only country whose name in English begins with the letter 'O'?
Which African country was once called Abyssinia?
Lake Baikal, the world's deepest lake, is located in which country?
questions.explanation: It's also the oldest lake — about 25 million years.

questions.text: What is the only sea on Earth with no coastline?
questions.explanation: Bounded by ocean currents rather than land — within the North Atlantic Gyre.

questions.text: Istanbul straddles two continents. Which two?
Reno, Nevada lies farther west than Los Angeles, California.
questions.explanation: Yes — California curves out to the east, so Reno (at ~119.8°W) sits west of LA (~118.2°W).

questions.text: What is the world's smallest country by population (about 800 people)?
Which U.S. state has the longest coastline (including all tidal shoreline)?
questions.explanation: Alaska's tidal coastline is about 33,000 miles — more than the rest of the U.S. combined.

questions.text: What was the name of the first artificial satellite launched into Earth's orbit?
questions.explanation: Launched by the Soviet Union on October 4, 1957.

questions.text: Who was the longest-reigning British monarch before Queen Elizabeth II?
questions.explanation: 63 years, 7 months. Elizabeth II reigned 70 years.

questions.text: The Rosetta Stone was inscribed in three scripts: Greek, Demotic, and what?
The Eiffel Tower was originally intended to be a temporary structure.
questions.explanation: It was built for the 1889 World's Fair and was scheduled for demolition after 20 years — saved by its usefulness for radio transmission.

questions.text: Which ancient wonder of the world was located in Egypt?
questions.explanation: Both the Great Pyramid and the Lighthouse were in Egypt — but the lighthouse is the more pop trivia answer.

questions.text: What 1788 publication co-authored by Hamilton, Madison, and Jay argued for ratifying the U.S. Constitution?
In what year did the United States buy Alaska from Russia, and for how much?
questions.explanation: About 2 cents an acre — known then as 'Seward's Folly'.

questions.text: Who was U.S. President when women won the right to vote nationally?
questions.explanation: The 19th Amendment was ratified in August 1920 during Wilson's presidency.

questions.text: In the original SI base units, what was the basis for the kilogram before 2019?
questions.explanation: 'Le Grand K' — a single physical artifact in France — until redefined via Planck's constant in 2019.

questions.text: What is the most abundant element by mass in the human body?
questions.explanation: About 65% — mostly bound in water.

questions.text: Which scientist proposed the model of the atom as a 'plum pudding'?
questions.explanation: In 1904, after discovering the electron — soon overturned by Rutherford's gold-foil experiment.

questions.text: Antarctica is technically the world's largest desert.
questions.explanation: By precipitation, Antarctica counts as a polar desert — and is larger than the Sahara.

questions.text: What is the rarest naturally occurring element on Earth?
questions.explanation: Estimated less than 30 grams exist in Earth's crust at any time.

questions.text: Which planet has the fastest-spinning day in our solar system?
questions.explanation: ~9 hours, 56 minutes for one full rotation.

questions.text: What is the speed of sound in dry air at sea level (approx)?
questions.explanation: ~343 m/s at 20°C — depends on temperature and humidity.

questions.text: A coastline's measured length is finite and well-defined.
questions.explanation: The 'coastline paradox' — the more finely you measure, the longer it gets (related to fractal geometry).

questions.text: Which Beatles album was the first to feature lyrics printed on the cover?
options: Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band
questions.correct_answer: Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band

questions.text: What is the name of the fictional company in the show 'The Office'?
In 'The Godfather', what's the name of the family business front?
Which director has won the most Best Director Oscars (4)?
What was the highest-grossing film of all time before 'Avatar' released in 2009?
Drake's debut studio album was 'Take Care'.
questions.explanation: His debut studio album was 'Thank Me Later' (2010). 'Take Care' came in 2011.

questions.text: Which actor turned down the role of Neo in 'The Matrix' before Keanu Reeves was cast?
questions.explanation: Will Smith chose 'Wild Wild West' instead — a decision he later joked about.

questions.text: 'Stairway to Heaven' was released on which Led Zeppelin album?
questions.explanation: Untitled but commonly called 'IV', released November 1971.

questions.text: Which boxer was once known as 'The Ambling Alp'?
In golf, what is the term for a hole completed in two strokes under par?
How many dimples does a standard golf ball typically have?
questions.explanation: Most have between 300 and 500 — 336 is the most common.

questions.text: Which NBA player holds the record for points scored in a single game (100)?
questions.explanation: March 2, 1962 — Philadelphia Warriors vs. New York Knicks.

questions.text: What is the diameter of a basketball hoop, in inches?
questions.explanation: 18 inches across — a regulation ball is 9.5 inches.

questions.text: The first modern Olympic Games (1896) included women.
questions.explanation: Women weren't included until the 1900 Paris Games.

questions.text: In tennis, what is the name for a score of zero?
questions.explanation: Possibly from the French 'l'œuf' (the egg) — shaped like a zero.

questions.text: What's the only U.S. state that produces coffee commercially?
questions.explanation: Kona coffee, primarily on the Big Island.

questions.text: What spirit is the main ingredient in a mojito?
Aged Parmigiano-Reggiano is grated finer than what spice (which gave it the alternate name 'King of Cheeses')?
Brown sugar is just white sugar with molasses added.
questions.explanation: Light brown is ~3.5% molasses, dark brown ~6.5%.

questions.text: What gives sourdough bread its sour flavor?
questions.explanation: Wild lactobacillus bacteria produce lactic and acetic acids.

questions.text: What is the French term for the cooking technique of slow-cooking food in fat at low temperature?
Which is the longest word in English (45 letters), referring to a lung disease?
Who wrote 'One Hundred Years of Solitude'?
In which Shakespeare play does the line 'All that glitters is not gold' appear?
questions.explanation: Actually written as 'All that glisters is not gold' — Act II, Scene 7.

questions.text: The word 'set' has the most definitions of any English word.
questions.explanation: The OED lists more than 430 distinct senses.

questions.text: What's the name of a sentence that uses every letter of the alphabet?
questions.explanation: Like 'The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.'

questions.text: What instrument family is the saxophone in, despite being made of brass?
questions.explanation: It uses a single reed, putting it in the woodwind family.

questions.text: How many keys are on a standard full-size piano?
questions.explanation: 52 white + 36 black = 88 keys.

questions.text: What is the tempo marking 'allegro' approximately?
Mozart wrote his first symphony at age 8.
questions.explanation: Symphony No. 1 in E-flat major (1764).

questions.text: What is the next number in this sequence: 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, ?
questions.explanation: Fibonacci sequence — each number is the sum of the two before.

questions.text: What is the smallest prime number greater than 50?
How many sides does a dodecagon have?
Zero is a natural number in some mathematical conventions.
questions.explanation: Whether zero is 'natural' depends on the convention — both views are used.

questions.text: What's the unofficial sport of every Drake family gathering?
This tournament is, in fact, scored.
